# Land Tenure Arrangements in Ghana

The results from Table 2, together with the crop-level results (Tables S1--S4), reveal a land ownership structure in Ghana that is both dynamic and gradually evolving. Between 2012/13 and 2016/17, both landholding and effective ownership rights weakened. The results point to a gradual shift toward more informal and flexible land access arrangements, with growing reliance on customary systems and sharecropping.

At the aggregate level, the share of farmers operating on land they do not own rose from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Not owned","c1")`% in 2012/13 to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Not owned","c2")`% in 2016/17, while ownership without formal documentation declined from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Owned without documentation","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Owned without documentation","c2")`%. Ownership with formal documentation increased only modestly, from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Owned with documentation","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Owned with documentation","c2")`%. Reported ownership therefore contracted overall, and the modest growth in documented holdings did not offset the decline in undocumented ownership or the expansion of non-ownership arrangements.

This divergence becomes even more apparent when ownership rights are considered. Over the same period, the proportion of farmers reporting no ownership rights increased from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","No rights","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","No rights","c2")`%, pointing to a widening gap between nominal ownership and effective control over land. At the same time, access to key economic rights weakened. The share of farmers able to use land as collateral fell from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Can be used as collateral security","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Can be used as collateral security","c2")`%, while those able to sell land declined from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Can be sold","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Can be sold","c2")`%. Similarly, the proportion of farmers holding both sale and collateral rights dropped from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Can be used as collateral security and sold","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Can be used as collateral security and sold","c2")`%. These patterns suggest that although more farmers report owning land, fewer are able to exercise the types of rights that matter for long-term investment, access to finance, and land-based decision-making.

The increase in land tenure insecurity can be linked to a combination of institutional and structural factors within Ghana's land sector. Although reforms such as the Land Administration Project (Phase II, 2011--2018) were intended to improve land registration systems and strengthen tenure security, their implementation remained uneven, especially in rural areas where customary land systems still dominate [@Ghebru2017; @Barnes2026; @Mwamba2024]. As a result, many farmers continued to operate without formal documentation, limiting their ability to transfer land or use it as collateral [@Ghebru2017; @Barnes2026]. In addition, rising population pressure, land fragmentation, and the expansion of commercial agriculture intensified competition for land and increased reliance on informal arrangements such as family allocation, borrowing, and sharecropping [@Ubink2007; @Aslam2025; @Dhakal2025; @Obayelu2017]. Weak rural credit markets further reduced the economic value of land ownership, since customary holdings are rarely accepted as collateral.

Land acquisition patterns reinforce this broader picture. A majority of farmers accessed land through family or village allocation, although this declined only slightly from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Distributed by village/family","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Distributed by village/family","c2")`%, indicating the continued dominance of customary systems. Access to land free of charge declined from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Use free of charge","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Use free of charge","c2")`%, although such informal, socially mediated access remains sizeable. Sharecropping arrangements also increased from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Sharecropping","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Sharecropping","c2")`%, indicating a growing dependence on flexible but potentially less secure forms of land access. Although rental markets remained relatively small, they changed only modestly, while land purchases declined from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Purchased","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","Purchased","c2")`%, suggesting that formal land market transactions remain limited.

The distribution of sharecropping intensity points in the same direction. The proportion of farmers not engaged in sharecropping declined from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","0","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","0","c2")`%, while the share under moderate-intensity arrangements (1-49% of land) rose from `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","1-49","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","1-49","c2")`% and high-intensity arrangements (50-100%) held roughly steady (`r tbl_pct("table2.csv","50-100","c1")`% to `r tbl_pct("table2.csv","50-100","c2")`%). Although most farmers still operate outside sharecropping systems, a growing minority depend on such arrangements for land access. In this sense, land access is becoming more flexible, but not necessarily more secure.

The crop-level results (Tables S1--S4) show how these changes play out across different production systems. Informal ownership, particularly ownership without formal documentation, remains widespread among staple crop producers. It is especially high among beans (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Beans","c2")`%), sorghum (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Sorghum","c2")`%), millet (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Millet","c2")`%), and rice (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Rice","c2")`%), suggesting that farmers engaged in subsistence-oriented production are among the most exposed to insecure ownership conditions. By comparison, crops associated with higher market value and longer-term investment, such as cocoa (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Cocoa","c3")`%), banana (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Banana","c3")`%), and plantain (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Plantain","c3")`%), show relatively higher levels of documented ownership. This likely reflects the greater need for stronger tenure security in perennial and commercially oriented production systems, where investment periods are longer and returns depend on sustained control over land.

A similar pattern is observed when examining non-ownership across crop groups. Farming on land not owned by the household is particularly common among producers of banana (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Banana","c1")`%), cocoyam (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Cocoyam","c1")`%), and cassava (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Cassava","c1")`%), indicating a greater reliance on borrowed, rented, or sharecropped land for these crops. In contrast, cereals such as sorghum (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Sorghum","c1")`%) and millet (`r tbl_pct("tableS1.csv","Millet","c1")`%) show lower levels of non-ownership, suggesting more direct control over land in the areas where these crops dominate. These differences likely reflect both agroecological conditions and the continued influence of customary inheritance systems in shaping land distribution.

The mode of land acquisition also varies considerably across crops. Overall, `r tbl_pct("tableS2.csv","All crops listed","c1")`% of farmers accessed land through family or village distribution, but this share rises to `r tbl_pct("tableS2.csv","Millet","c1")`% for millet, `r tbl_pct("tableS2.csv","Sorghum","c1")`% for sorghum, and `r tbl_pct("tableS2.csv","Rice","c1")`% for rice, indicating the importance of communal allocation in staple production systems. Access to land free of charge is especially common among peanuts (`r tbl_pct("tableS2.csv","Peanut","c2")`%) and yam (`r tbl_pct("tableS2.csv","Yam","c2")`%), again pointing to the importance of informal and temporary arrangements. Sharecropping is more prevalent among banana (`r tbl_pct("tableS2.csv","Banana","c3")`%), cocoa (`r tbl_pct("tableS2.csv","Cocoa","c3")`%), and palm (`r tbl_pct("tableS2.csv","Palm","c3")`%) producers, suggesting that such arrangements are particularly useful in crop systems that require higher labour and capital inputs. Although still less common overall, rental and purchase arrangements are relatively more visible among producers of tomato, eggplant, and cocoa, indicating a gradual movement toward more market-based land access in parts of the agricultural sector.

The crop-specific distribution of ownership rights provides further evidence of uneven tenure security. Large proportions of millet (`r tbl_pct("tableS3.csv","Millet","c1")`%), sorghum (`r tbl_pct("tableS3.csv","Sorghum","c1")`%), and beans (`r tbl_pct("tableS3.csv","Beans","c1")`%) producers report having no formal ownership rights, and only very small shares can use land as collateral or sell it. This points to weak enforceability of rights in staple crop systems. By comparison, producers of cocoa (`r tbl_pct("tableS3.csv","Cocoa","c4")`%) and palm (`r tbl_pct("tableS3.csv","Palm","c4")`%) exhibit relatively stronger ownership security, with greater access to transfer and collateral rights. This suggests that secure and transferable land rights remain concentrated among farmers engaged in higher-value, commercially oriented, and longer-term agricultural production.

The role of sharecropping also varies substantially across crops. While the majority of farmers overall (`r tbl_pct("tableS4.csv","All crops listed","c1")`%) cultivate land outside sharecropping systems, high-intensity sharecropping is particularly common among banana (`r tbl_pct("tableS4.csv","Banana","c3")`%), cocoyam (`r tbl_pct("tableS4.csv","Cocoyam","c3")`%), and palm (`r tbl_pct("tableS4.csv","Palm","c3")`%) producers. In these cases, sharecropping appears to serve as an important mechanism for facilitating access to land in crop systems that require more labour and capital. By contrast, staple crops such as millet (`r tbl_pct("tableS4.csv","Millet","c1")`% non-sharecropped), sorghum (`r tbl_pct("tableS4.csv","Sorghum","c1")`%), and peanuts (`r tbl_pct("tableS4.csv","Peanut","c1")`%) show very limited reliance on sharecropping, reflecting stronger dependence on direct or customary land access.

Overall, the combined evidence from Table 2 and Tables S1--S4 points to a land ownership system in transition. Reported ownership is contracting, effective ownership rights are weakening, and informal, flexible, and often less secure access arrangements are becoming increasingly important. At the same time, strong and transferable land rights remain concentrated among farmers engaged in higher-value and longer-term crop production. This dual structure highlights persistent inequalities in land access and ownership security, with important implications for agricultural investment, productivity, and rural development.
